title.en: The Tusked Pig That Heralds the Bumper Harvest

summary.zh: 钦山的瑞兽，形状像猪而长着獠牙，叫声像在喊自己的名字；它一出现，天下就要大丰收。
summary.en: An auspicious beast of Qin Mountain, shaped like a pig with prominent tusks, its cry naming itself. When it appears, the world is due a great harvest.

description.zh:
东次四经的钦山多金玉而不产石头。山中有兽名当康：形状是一头猪，却生着外露的獠牙，叫起来像在喊自己的名字。它的兆应写作“见则天下大穰”——当康现身之年，天下五谷丰登。

在灾异密布的东次四经里，当康是唯一的好消息。它的邻居们个个面目狰狞：北号之山的猲狙食人，剡山的合窳带来大水，太山的蜚带来大疫，女烝之山的薄鱼带来大旱。唯独这头有牙的猪，扛着“大穰”二字行走。以猪为丰年之兆并不难解：豕是六畜之一、祭祀与年节的主角，仓廪实则猪圈满，一头膘肥牙壮的野豚，本身就是好年成的样子。

历代图本多画当康作奔走回首状，獠牙上翘，体态浑圆，几乎带着喜气。后世谈《山海经》祥瑞，兽类常举当康与狪狪并称——同为豕形，一个报丰年，一个怀珍珠，东山经把两件好事都派给了猪。
description.en:
Qin Mountain, in the fourth eastern guideway, abounds in gold and jade and has no common stone at all. Its beast is the dangkang: a pig in shape, but with tusks protruding, and a cry that sounds like its own name. Its omen reads 'when it is seen, all under heaven enjoys a great harvest' — the year the dangkang shows itself, the granaries fill.

In the disaster-ridden fourth eastern guideway, the dangkang is the only piece of good news. Its neighbours are uniformly grim: the man-eating xieju of Beihao Mountain, the flood-bringing heyu of Yan Mountain, the plague-bearing fei of Tai Mountain, the drought-fish of Nüzheng Mountain. Alone among them, the tusked pig walks under the sign of abundance. A pig as harvest omen needs little explaining: swine head the sacrificial and festival table among the six domestic animals, and full granaries mean full sties — a fat, strong-tusked boar simply looks like a good year.

Illustrated editions favour a running dangkang glancing back over its shoulder, tusks upturned, body round, almost festive. Later writers on the classic's auspicious beasts habitually pair it with the tongtong — both pig-shaped, one announcing plenty, the other carrying pearls. The eastern mountains assigned both of their blessings to pigs.

value.en: Its appearance brings a great harvest under heaven

sources.zh: 《山海经·东山经》（东次四经·钦山）
sources.en: Classic of Mountains and Seas, 'Eastern Mountains' (Qin Mountain)
